author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
Man Does No Good Thing Which God Does Not Cause Him to Do.
title: Man Does No Good Thing Which God Does Not Cause Him to Do.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Wherefore God does many good things in man which man does not do; but man does none which God does not cause man to do.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Accordingly, there would be no desire of good in man from the Lord if it were not a good; but if it is a good, we have it not save from Him who is supremely and incommunicably good.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For what is the desire for good but love, of which John the apostle speaks without any ambiguity, and says, “Love is of God”?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Nor is its beginning of ourselves, and its perfection of God; but if love is of God, we have the whole of it from God.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: May God by all means turn away this folly of making ourselves first in His gifts, Himself last,—because “His mercy shall prevent me.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And it is He to whom is faithfully and truthfully sung, “For Thou hast prevented him with the blessings of sweetness.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And what is here more fitly understood than that very desire of good of which we are speaking?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For good begins then to be longed for when it has begun to grow sweet.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But when good is done by the fear of penalty, not by the love of righteousness, good is not yet well done.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Nor is that done in the heart which seems to be done in the act when a man would rather not do it if he could evade it with impunity.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Therefore the “blessing of sweetness” is God’s grace, by which is caused in us that what He prescribes to us delights us, and we desire it,—that is, we love it; in which if God does not precede us, not only is it not perfected, but it is not even begun, from us.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For, if without Him we are able to do nothing actually, we are able neither to begin nor to perfect,—because to begin, it is said “His mercy shall prevent me;” to finish, it is said, “His mercy shall follow me.”
